weak_signal: Centralized-critic multi-agent actor–critic for multi-step summarization
what_it_was: Differentiable Multi-Agent Actor–Critic (DiMAC) for radiology report summarization used multiple extractive agents (for sentences and keywords) trained with an actor–critic framework and a differentiable communication channel, effectively a multi-agent RL system with a coordinating critic over text selection decisions.
why_weak_signal: It showed that critic-based methods can coordinate multiple textual decision-makers over long documents, hinting at later critic-based algorithms where centralized or shared critics evaluate complex multi-step LM behaviors (planning, tool use, multi-agent reasoning) rather than single-step bandit-style completions.

resource.paper_title: Differentiable Multi-Agent Actor-Critic for Multi-Step Radiology Report Summarization

weak_signal: Critic-guided offline LM training via self-generated data revision
what_it_was: GPT-Critic framed end-to-end task-oriented dialogue as offline RL with a GPT-based Q-value critic: a critic network (sharing transformer layers with GPT-2) scores candidate responses, the best candidate per turn is selected according to Q, and the policy is updated by behavior cloning on this critic-revised dataset, iterating critic training and policy improvement.
why_weak_signal: This was a clear demonstration of an LM actor guided by a learned critic in language space, without direct policy gradients, and it normalized the idea of critic-guided self-improvement—directly anticipating modern critic-based algorithms where value/Q critics (or critic-like LMs) drive dataset revision, policy optimization, and decoding strategies for large language models.

resource.paper_title: GPT-Critic: Offline Reinforcement Learning for End-to-End Task-Oriented Dialogue Systems

weak_signal: Self-critical baseline-only policy gradients for pretrained text generators
what_it_was: Work on self-critical sequence training revisited REINFORCE for sequence models, showing that you can cut gradient variance by using the model’s own greedy output as a baseline instead of learning a separate value function. Luo (2020) proposed a better variant of SCST with an improved baseline choice, and ReGen (EMNLP 2021) applied SCST to fine-tune large pretrained encoder–decoder LMs (e.g., T5) for both text-to-graph and graph-to-text generation, explicitly “employing the model itself as its own critic.”
why_weak_signal: These papers demonstrated that for strong sequence models (including PLMs), RL fine-tuning could be stable and performant using only reward-weighted log-likelihood with a cleverly chosen baseline, with no learned value network at all. That directly foreshadows critic-free RLHF-style methods (e.g., REINFORCE/RLOO/GRPO) where advantages are computed from simple baselines or group statistics instead of training a critic head.

resource.paper_title: Self-Critical Sequence Training for Image Captioning
How to Avoid the Pitfalls of Self-Critical Training
ReGen: Reinforcement Learning for Text and Knowledge Base Generation using Pretrained Language Models

weak_signal: Variance‑reduced multi-sample REINFORCE (leave-one-out + control variates) for discrete models
what_it_was: Titsias & Shi introduced “double control variates” for the REINFORCE leave‑one‑out estimator, showing that with multiple samples per input and carefully constructed baselines you can dramatically reduce variance when training discrete latent-variable models, while keeping the estimator unbiased and avoiding any extra cost beyond REINFORCE‑LOO itself. Follow‑up work on discrete Stein operators (RODEO, NeurIPS 2022) built flexible, learnable control variates on top of REINFORCE‑LOO, again targeting low‑variance score-function gradients in discrete settings.
why_weak_signal: These papers crystallized a core recipe: use many samples per context plus sophisticated baselines/control variates to make pure policy-gradient estimators competitive, without any value function. Modern critic-free RLHF algorithms (RLOO, GRPO, REINFORCE++-style methods) essentially instantiate this recipe in NLP by drawing multiple completions per prompt and computing advantages from sample-based statistics instead of training a critic network.

resource.paper_title: Estimating Gradients for Discrete Random Variables by Sampling without Replacement

Double Control Variates for Gradient Estimation in Discrete Latent Variable Models

weak_signal: Offline counterfactual bandit training from logged human feedback in sequence-to-sequence tasks
what_it_was: Kreutzer et al. framed real-world MT and other seq2seq systems as offline reinforcement learning from human interaction logs: they used counterfactual risk minimization and importance-weighted policy gradients to directly optimize sequence-level rewards from logged bandit feedback, highlighting practical issues like degenerate rewards, off-policy bias, and logging-policy constraints.
why_weak_signal: This line of work made “learning from human feedback” in NLP look much more like bandit policy optimization than full RL, with an objective that is essentially a critic-free REINFORCE-style loss over logged data. Critic-free RLHF methods later adopt exactly this perspective—treating prompts as contexts, completions as bandit actions, and optimizing a bandit objective from rewards or reward-model scores, typically with only baselines and KL terms rather than any value network.

resource.paper_title: Bandits Don't Follow Rules: Balancing Multi-Facet Machine Translation with Multi-Reward Training

weak_signal: Policy-gradient NMT reframed as a bandit optimization problem of exploration and reward scaling
what_it_was: “Revisiting the Weaknesses of Reinforcement Learning for Neural Machine Translation” systematically probed why RL sometimes “fails” for NMT and showed that many issues disappear when you tune exploration, reward scaling, and baselines properly. The study used classic policy-gradient algorithms (REINFORCE variants, MIXER, SCST) and a variety of rewards, but did not lean on actor–critic architectures—demonstrating that simple policy gradients could be effective if you get the bandit setup and normalization right.
why_weak_signal: By arguing that stability and performance in text RL depend more on exploration and reward normalization than on sophisticated critics, this work implicitly downgraded the “critic” as the central solution concept. That opened the door to later critic-free RLHF designs where the main levers are sampling strategy, group-based advantages, and reward/KL scaling—while the value head is dropped entirely.

resource.paper_title: Revisiting the Weaknesses of Reinforcement Learning for Neural Machine Translation

weak_signal: Semantic‑reward self-critical REINFORCE for question summarization
what_it_was: Yadav et al. cast medical question summarization as an RL problem over a Transformer encoder–decoder, defining learned semantic rewards from BERT-based classifiers for question type and question focus, then optimizing a mixed objective with REINFORCE and a self‑critical (greedy) baseline instead of any critic network. The gradient is just (reward − baseline) times log‑probability of the sampled summary, with SCST providing variance reduction.
why_weak_signal: This work showed that you can plug rich, model-based semantic rewards (very close in spirit to modern reward models) into a pure policy-gradient + baseline setup and get SOTA results—no value function required. That is exactly the structural pattern of today’s critic-free RLHF algorithms, which combine learned reward models, bandit-style policy gradients, and clever baselines, while omitting a separate critic head.

weak_signal: Offline RL from human interaction logs for sequence-to-sequence models
what_it_was: Kreutzer et al. examined real-world MT and semantic parsing logs (input, model output, human quality score) as training data, explicitly casting them as an off-policy RL setting where a logging policy produces outputs, users give bandit feedback, and learning uses counterfactual objectives such as IPS, deterministic propensity matching, and doubly-robust estimators under deterministic logging and strong bias.
why_weak_signal: This paper elevated the off-policy structure—logging policy vs. target policy vs. logged rewards—to a first-class design object in NLP and mapped out how to stabilize optimization under biased logs, foreshadowing later off-policy optimization for LMs that rely on static preference datasets and counterfactual bandit-style objectives rather than interactive rollouts.

resource.paper_title: Bandits Don't Follow Rules: Balancing Multi-Facet Machine Translation with Multi-Reward Training

weak_signal: Off-policy demonstration learning for text generation with importance-weighted tokens
what_it_was: Pang & He reframed supervised text generation as offline, off-policy RL from expert demonstrations, proposing GOLD: a learning scheme that reweights reference tokens using an importance-ratio analogue so the model can learn from demonstrations while accounting for its own sampled histories, all without online data collection.
why_weak_signal: GOLD showed that off-policy RL can collapse into a reweighted log-likelihood objective on static demonstrations, giving a concrete solution template where off-policy optimization looks like modified cross-entropy—an early analogue to later LM methods that train new policies purely from fixed demonstration/preference data via reweighting and KL-style constraints.

resource.paper_title: Text Generation by Learning from Demonstrations

weak_signal: KL-controlled pessimistic offline RL for dialogue from static human feedback
what_it_was: Jaques et al. collected open-domain chat logs with implicit human feedback (e.g., sentiment, laughter, similarity cues) and introduced an offline RL algorithm that constrains the learned dialogue policy to stay close to a pretrained LM via KL-control, while adding pessimism in value estimation to avoid over-optimistic extrapolation when training purely from logged conversations.
why_weak_signal: This work provided a concrete solution recipe for off-policy tuning of conversational agents—optimize a new policy on static feedback logs while regularizing toward a prior LM—anticipating modern off-policy alignment methods where LMs are optimized from fixed preference/feedback data under strong KL or prior constraints instead of interactive RL.

resource.paper_title: Human-Centric Dialog Training via Offline Reinforcement Learning

weak_signal: Implicit language Q-learning for offline RL on language-model trajectories
what_it_was: Snell et al. introduced Implicit Language Q-Learning (ILQL), an offline RL algorithm for LMs that adapts implicit Q-learning to token-level POMDPs: Q and V functions are trained with conservative Bellman backups on static rewarded trajectories, and a new policy is extracted by perturbing a pretrained LM’s logits with these learned values to maximize user-specified utilities without online interaction.
why_weak_signal: ILQL turned off-policy optimization into a practical LM workflow—fit Q/V on logged data, then decode under those values—showing how static preference or task datasets can be used to steer models via offline RL, which directly anticipates off-policy optimization becoming a mainstream alternative to online RLHF for large language models.
